allergens: gluten (chickpea flour may be cross-contaminated; besan itself is gluten-free but verify)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of paneer pakora is approximately 4 pieces weighing around 100 g, commonly served as a snack or starter with mint chutney or tamarind sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Pakora — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Paneer Pakora (cr_id G00010783), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — chickpea flour (besan) based fried snack analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer pakora in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 48 (low) based on the closest analogous food: chickpea flour (besan) based fried pakora/bhajia, which has a published GI of approximately 45–52 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021; Foster-Powell et al.). The chickpea flour batter is the dominant carbohydrate contributor; paneer contributes protein and fat which further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 16.24 × 1.0) / 100 ≈ 7.8, rounded to 7.7 for a 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100 g with deep-fried saturated fat burden
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 380 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 50–60 g, avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted chutneys, and prefer air-fried or baked preparation to reduce the saturated fat contribution to arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 16.24 g/100 g attenuated by chickpea flour fibre 3.2 g/100 g and protein 4.92 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The chickpea flour (besan) batter provides 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g which moderates post-prandial glucose absorption; the estimated GI of 48 (low) and glycemic load of 7.7 per 100 g serving are acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes when portion is limited to 3–4 pieces (approximately 80–100 g) and paired with a non-starchy vegetable side.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with saturated fat ~1.8 g/100 g and trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying introduces saturated fat and trace trans fat that are pro-atherogenic; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict intake to occasional small portions (≤80 g), choose air-fried or oven-baked preparation, and monitor total daily saturated fat intake in the context of the full dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trace trans fat that are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis management due to AGE formation and trace trans fat; patients should substitute with air-fried or baked paneer seasoned with turmeric and avoid nightshade-containing spice blends if nightshade-sensitive.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Pakora (Hindi: पनीर पकोड़ा) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made by coating cubes or slices of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) in a spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter and deep-frying until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy and lightly spiced with turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, and salt, while the interior remains soft, milky, and slightly chewy from the paneer. It is widely consumed across India and in the South Asian diaspora in Singapore as a street food, tea-time snack, or starter at restaurants and hawker stalls. Per 100 g, paneer pakora provides 125 kcal, 4.92 g protein, 4.77 g fat, 16.24 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 380 mg sodium. The chickpea flour batter contributes plant protein and soluble fibre, which moderate the glycaemic response (estimated GI 48, low). The deep-fried preparation increases caloric density and introduces saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), making it a moderate-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. For Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI and fibre content make it acceptable in controlled portions. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Common accompaniments include mint-coriander chutney, tamarind chutney, and masala chai.
common_user_queries: Is paneer pakora good for diabetics?
Can I eat paneer pakora with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010783) with a confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature for chickpea flour-based fried snacks, which is the closest defensible match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for paneer pakora is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. 2. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on the input health_suitability caution noting trace trans fat from deep-frying oil; this is consistent with USDA data for deep-fried chickpea flour products. 3. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried besan-battered snacks (USDA SR Legacy #16057 chickpea flour, adjusted for frying oil absorption); not directly reported in HPB entry. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; paneer is a dairy product and typically contains some cholesterol, but the HPB source reports 0.0 — this is accepted as confirmed zero per the approved source. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for paneer pakora as of 2026-04-16. 6. food_group corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Snacks' and food_subgroup from 'Baking ingredients' to 'Deep-fried snacks' as paneer pakora is a prepared deep-fried snack dish, not a raw baking ingredient. 7. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for paneer pakora is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16.
